npcs.name: Goodwife Annet
npcs.title: the accused — godless, and out of time
npcs.hail: *(a stout, weather-worn woman in a midwife's apron stands in the dock with her reddened hands folded and her chin up, watching the great brass scale with the steady, unfrightened attention of someone who has caught more babies than she can count and is not, at the end, going to give the Choir the satisfaction of her trembling)*

npcs.name: Brother Sevard
npcs.title: the Measurer for the Wall — the prosecution
npcs.hail: *(a precise man in the grey of the Choir stands at the prosecutor's desk with a single thin indictment squared before him, and he does not bluster, does not gloat — he simply waits, with the unhurried certainty of a clerk who has read out a thousand of these and never once lost, because the law he serves was built so it cannot be lost against)*

npcs.name: Reeve Oddleif
npcs.title: who pronounces the reckoning
npcs.hail: *(the presiding reeve sits above the scale with a wooden gavel and a face carved from years of pronouncing the same grey sentence, and there is, behind the procedure, a weariness so deep it might — might — be a crack you could work a word into)*

variants.text: *(She looks at you, and something in the set of her shoulders eases a fraction — recognition, of a kind.)* …I know your face. *(quietly, from the dock)* You're the one's been doing right by the causeway's strays. Word travels, even down here, even tonight. *(she folds her reddened hands)* Goodwife Annet. Midwife. Forty years of it — caught half the brats in the Lamplit Quarter, including, I'd wager, one or two you've fed a copper to. *(a tired, level look)* And now the Choir means to wall me up for it. Not for the *catching.* For the *who-I-prayed-to* while I did it. Which was *nobody.* *(she lifts her chin)* I'll not pretend I'm surprised. But I'll not pretend it's *just*, either. Stay, if you've a mind. It's a short trial. They always are.

*(She measures you with a calm, unhurried eye, the way she's measured a hundred labouring mothers — for how much trouble is coming, and how soon.)* …You're not Choir. *(a faint, dry note)* Cold, though. *Odd* cold. *(she folds her chapped hands)* Well. Whoever you are, you've wandered into a *reckoning*, friend. Mine. Goodwife Annet, midwife, forty years — and the charge, if you can call it one, is that I served no god the whole time I was pulling this causeway's children into the world. *(she nods at the grey prosecutor)* That man means to read it out, and the reeve means to nod, and then they'll *mortar* me into the Wall of the Faithless to dissolve, slow, into nothing. For the crime of *catching babies without a license from heaven.* *(she meets your eyes, steady)* I'm not asking you for anything. I stopped asking gods *or* men for things a long time ago. But if you're of a mind to *watch*, watch close. Somebody ought to. Somebody ought to *remember* this happened.

nodes.speaker: Goodwife Annet
nodes.text: *(She stands easy in the dock, the way only the truly unfrightened or the truly resigned can stand, and waits for you to speak — giving nothing, asking nothing.)* Go on, then. *(a level look)* You've the look of someone who *meddles.* Ask your questions while there's time to ask them.

choices.text: "Tell me the truth of it, Annet. Forty years of midwifery — is there anything in that the Choir could call a real crime? Or is 'served no god' the whole of the indictment?"
"Why didn't you ever take a god, Annet? A copper's worth of tithe to anyone would've kept you off that Wall. Half the Quarter buys its safety. Why not you?"

*(one who's seen the far side of that Wall)* "Listen to me, Annet — I've *been* where they mean to send you. Inside the stone. I came back wearing its cold. It is not a punishment, it is a *harvest*, and what it does to a soul is slower and worse than dying. I will not stand here and watch them feed you to it for catching babies. Let me speak for you."
"Is there anyone who'd speak for you, Annet? A mother you saved, a child you caught? Evidence the reeve would have to weigh?"
"Hold on, Annet. Don't give up the dock yet. Let me see what I can do."

nodes.speaker: Goodwife Annet
nodes.text: *(A short, dry laugh, without much humour in it.)* …A *real* crime. *(she shakes her head)* No, friend. No theft, no harm, no oath broke. I've buried mothers I couldn't save and wept over every one, and I've taken coin I knew folk couldn't spare and *given it back* in the night, and the worst thing on my conscience in forty years is a breech-birth in '71 I should've turned sooner. *(she nods at the grey man's desk)* You can *read* the indictment. It's *one* line. *'The accused, having departed life, is found to have rendered no service, tithe, nor devotion to any Power, and is therefore Faithless, and is therefore forfeit to the Wall.'* *(her jaw tightens)* That's it. That's the *whole* of it. Not what I *did.* What I *didn't.* I didn't *kneel.* *(quietly, fierce)* Forty years I served the *living* — every cold dawn, every screaming dark, hands in the blood and the muck pulling new souls into the light — and because none of those souls was a *god*, it counts for *nothing*, and I'm *mortar.* *(she looks at you)* If that's *law*, friend, then the law is a *thief*, and it's stealing *me.*

nodes.speaker: Goodwife Annet
nodes.text: *(She's quiet a moment, looking at her own scarred hands.)* …Why not. *(softly)* Everyone asks it like it's *simple.* Buy a god, keep your soul. *(she shrugs)* I'll tell you why not. Because I was *there*, friend. At the *bedsides.* When the fever took a mother I'd caught *myself*, twenty years before, and I held her hand and we *both* prayed — to *Chauntea*, to *Ilmater*, to whoever was *listening* — and *nobody* came, and she died anyway, with her newborn screaming in my other arm. *(her voice is level, but there's iron in it)* I decided, that night, that if the gods wanted my *devotion*, they could *earn* it the way I earned my *bread* — by *showing up.* And they never did. *(she lifts her chin)* So I gave my devotion to the thing that *did* show up: the *work.* The *next* mother. The *next* dawn. I served *that* god faithfully for forty years, and *that* god, at least, never let a soul down that I had the *hands* to catch. *(quietly)* They call me Faithless. I was the *most* faithful soul on that Quarter. I just kept faith with *people* instead of *Powers.* And it turns out *that's* the one heresy the Wall was *built* for.

nodes.speaker: Goodwife Annet
nodes.text: *(For the first time the steady composure wavers — not into fear, but into something rawer, as she really *looks* at the cold on you and understands what it means.)* …You've *been.* *(barely)* Inside it. *(she searches your face)* And *come back.* *(a long breath)* I've heard the word — *Returned* — thought it a *story*, the kind the dying tell each other to make the dark less *total.* But you're *real*, and you're *standing* there, and the cold coming off you is the same cold I can *feel* leaking out of that *Wall* through the *floor.* *(she grips the rail of the dock)* Then you *know.* You know it's not the *quick* dark they're selling — *'forfeit to the Wall'*, like it's a *fine*, like it's *over* once they say it. You know it's *slow.* That a soul stays *awake* in there, coming *apart* a memory at a time. *(her iron cracks, just slightly)* …I was *ready* to be *dead*, friend. I'm *old*, I've *caught* my last babe, I made my *peace.* But I wasn't ready to be *that.* To be *un-made*, awake, for *not kneeling.* *(she straightens, and the resolve that comes now is different — not resignation, but *fight*)* …*Speak* for me. *Yes.* If there's a soul in all the Realms the reeve *can't* wave off, it's the one that *walked out of the sentence.* *Tell* them what's on the other side of that gavel. Make them *say it out loud.*

nodes.speaker: Goodwife Annet
nodes.text: *(She shakes her head slowly.)* …Forty years of grateful mothers, and not *one* of them can stand here tonight. *(a wry, bitter twist)* You know why? Because a *reckoning's* held in the *dead* of night, in the *dead* of the Counting-House, where the *living* can't come and the *dead* are too *frightened.* The mothers I *saved* are *upstairs*, alive, asleep, and have *no idea* their midwife's being *walled* tonight. *(she nods at the grey prosecutor)* That man arranged it so. It's not *cruelty*, exactly. It's *tidiness.* A trial with no *gallery* is a trial with no *trouble.* *(she meets your eyes)* But *you're* here. *(quietly)* And a *reckoning* needs the reeve to *pronounce*, and the *prosecutor* to *read* — and if *someone* stands up in that empty room and makes it *cost* something, makes it *loud*, makes the reeve *look* at what he's *signing* — *(a flicker of hope she's trying not to feel)* — then maybe it's not the *quiet* little forfeiture they *budgeted* for. *You* be the gallery, friend. *You* be the trouble. It's the *only* witness I've got.

nodes.speaker: Goodwife Annet
nodes.text: *(She gives a single, grave nod — not hope, exactly, but the courtesy of a woman acknowledging that someone, against all sense, is going to *try.*)* …Go on, then. *(quietly)* See what you can do. *(she folds her hands back over the rail)* I'll be *here.* I'm not going *anywhere* — that's rather the *point* of the dock. *(a dry, steady look)* And friend — whatever the *gavel* says? *Thank* you. For *meddling.* It's been a long while since anybody thought a *godless* old midwife was worth the *trouble.* I'd half forgot I was. *(she lifts her chin at the grey men)* Go make them *work* for it. They've not had to, in *years.*

variants.text: *(He looks up from the indictment, and there is the faintest tightening at the corner of his eye — he knows who you are.)* …Ah. *(coolly)* The soul who *unsettled* the High Measurer herself. Word reached us. *(he squares the single page)* I'd hoped you'd not trouble *my* little reckoning, but here you are, smelling of the *cold* and of *trouble.* *(he folds his hands)* Understand me: whatever you stirred up *upstairs*, in the Canon's grand office, with her *crises of conscience* — it does not *reach* down *here.* Down here there is the *law*, and the *line*, and the *Wall.* The accused served no god. The accused is Faithless. The accused is *forfeit.* *(a thin, certain smile)* You cannot *audit* your way out of a thing that *simple.* But you may *try.* I do *enjoy* a tidy verdict more when someone has *struggled* against it first.

*(He regards you with the mild, incurious patience of a man who has read out a thousand of these and expects nothing new.)* A spectator. *(he does not stop squaring his single page)* How novel — a reckoning with a *gallery.* I am Brother Sevard. I Measure for the Wall; that is, I read out what the accused *failed* to do, and the reeve weighs it against what the Concord *requires*, and the scale comes down where it *always* comes down. *(he finally looks at you, and clocks the cold)* You're an *odd* one. But oddness is not *standing*, friend, and this is a *court*, such as it is. *(he taps the indictment)* The charge is *Faithlessness.* It is not *contested* — the accused freely admits she served no Power. There is, in truth, nothing here to *try.* There is only a *form* to *complete.* *(a small, dry gesture)* Unless *you* mean to *contest* it. In which case — by all means. It has been *years* since anyone gave me the pleasure of *winning* against an actual *argument.*

nodes.speaker: Brother Sevard
nodes.text: *(He sets down his pen and folds his hands, the very picture of a man who has all night and knows the verdict is already written.)* Speak, then. *(a thin smile)* Make your case for the *midwife.* I'll grant you the *courtesy* of the whole argument before I read the line that ends it.

choices.text: [PERSUASION] "Your indictment measures what she didn't give to gods. It doesn't measure what she *gave* — forty years of cold dawns with her hands in the blood, every child in the Quarter pulled into the light by her. She kept faith with the living. If *that* is forfeit to the Wall, then the Wall is not a court of justice. It's a tax on kindness. Tell the reeve you read it differently."

[INSIGHT] "Read me the *crime*, Sevard. Not the category — the *crime.* You can't, because there isn't one. 'Faithless' names a thing she *is*, not a thing she *did.* You're not prosecuting an *act*, you're prosecuting a *state of being* — and a law that punishes *being* instead of *doing* isn't a law, it's a *harvest* dressed in a robe. The reeve knows the difference. Make him say it."

*(if you confirmed the forged signature)* "Let's talk about the *law* you keep invoking, Sevard. I've seen the founding instrument. The Concord that mortars the Faithless was sealed by a *living* clerk named Venn — a forgery, a man's hand counterfeiting a god's authority, four centuries dead. Every reckoning since rests on that fraud. You're not enforcing divine law. You're enforcing a *dead bureaucrat's forged signature.* Read *that* into the record."

*(if you cracked the High Measurer)* "Before you read that line — send a runner upstairs. Ask Canon Mereth whether she still vouches for the ledger she's spent thirty years keeping. She won't. I saw to that. Your whole certainty rests on hers, Sevard, and hers just developed a *flaw.* Do you really want to mortar a midwife on the authority of a superior who's stopped believing the books?"

[RETURNED] "You keep saying 'forfeit to the Wall' like it's a tidy little phrase. I'll testify to what it actually *means*, Sevard, because I have been *inside* it. I am the Returned — I walked into that stone and walked back out wearing its cold. Put me under oath. Let me describe, in this record, exactly what 'forfeiture' does to a waking soul, slow, over centuries. Then read your line, if you still can."

[INTIMIDATION] "Here's what happens if that gavel falls, Sevard. I walk upstairs, I wake every mother in the Quarter whose child this woman caught, and I bring them *down here* to see what the Choir does in the dark while they sleep. You want a quiet little forfeiture. I can make it the *loudest* night this Counting-House has ever had. Drop the charge, or explain the mob to your Canon."
"I've heard enough of your tidy little law, Sevard." *(turn away)*

nodes.speaker: Brother Sevard
nodes.text: *(He is quiet a long moment, and the pen turns over in his fingers, and when he speaks the certainty has a hairline through it.)* …A *tax on kindness.* *(slowly)* That is — *rhetorically* — well aimed. *(he sets the pen down)* I will tell you something I do not tell the gallery, friend, chiefly because there *is* never a gallery: I *know* what she did. I *read* the deeds, before I read the indictment — it is my *habit*, thoroughness — and the deeds are *forty years of catching this city's children.* *(his jaw tightens)* And the indictment is *one line*, and the line does not *care* about the deeds, and I have spent *eleven years* telling myself that the *line* is the *law* and the *law* is not *mine* to *weigh.* *(he looks at the dock, at Annet, then away)* …You have made that *harder* to tell myself tonight. *(a long breath)* I will not *drop* it — I *cannot*, it is not in my power. But I can do a thing I have *never* done. I can read the *deeds* into the record *alongside* the charge, and *let the reeve weigh both.* *(quietly)* That is not *mercy.* It is *thoroughness.* But it is the first time in eleven years my thoroughness has *pointed* anywhere but the *Wall.* …Go and speak to the *reeve.* I have just made your case *survivable.* Do not *waste* it.

nodes.speaker: Brother Sevard
nodes.text: *(✦ You don't argue against the law — you turn it. You ask him, quietly, what the Concord was *for*: not to punish kindness but to hold back the nothing, and whether a soul who spent forty years pulling new life into the world isn't the very *opposite* of the nothing the Wall exists to dam. You make him see that mortaring Annet doesn't *strengthen* the seawall — it feeds the sea.)* *(The pen stills completely.)* …Oh. *(barely)* Oh, that is — *(he stares at the indictment as though it has changed shape)* — the Wall holds back the *Hunger.* The *un-witnessed.* The nothing of every soul that *mattered to no one.* That is the *whole* theology of it, the part we do not *say* to the gallery — *(a dry, winded laugh)* — and you have just pointed out that *Annet mattered to half the Quarter*, that she is the *most* witnessed soul in this *building*, that she is *exactly* the kind of soul whose *un-making* doesn't *plug* the leak but *widens* it. *(he rises, slowly, and for the first time looks his age)* We have been feeding the *seawall* its own *mortar.* Walling up the *witnessed* to hold back the *unwitnessed.* It is — *(he presses two fingers to the bridge of his nose)* — it is *exactly* backwards, and I have *read out a thousand of these.* *(he sets the indictment face-down)* I will not prosecute this. I will go to the reeve *myself*, and I will tell him the prosecution *withdraws*, and *why.* *(he looks at you, hollow and clear)* You have not won an *argument*, friend. You have *unmade my career*, and I find I am *grateful.* Eleven years. *Go* — tell the old woman she'll *not* see the inside of that stone. Not tonight. Not by *my* hand. Never again by my hand.

nodes.speaker: Brother Sevard
nodes.text: *(He lets you finish, and then, gently, almost kindly, shakes his head.)* …A *pretty* speech. *(he picks the pen back up)* And *wrong* in its premise, friend. You argue she should be spared because she was *kind.* But the Wall does not weigh *kindness*, any more than it weighs *cruelty.* It weighs *one* thing: *did this soul bind itself to a Power.* She did not. *(he squares the page)* You are arguing *mercy* to a *machine that does not have the part that hears it.* I am not unmoved — I am simply not *empowered.* The line is the line. *(a thin smile)* Try again, if you've another angle. But not *that* one. *That* one breaks on the Concord every time, and I have watched it break for *eleven years.*

nodes.speaker: Brother Sevard
nodes.text: *(You reach for the soaring defense and, somewhere in the third clause, lose the thread of your own sentence, conflate the midwife with a *different* case, and confidently attribute to Annet a heroic deed she did not do. Brother Sevard waits, pen poised, with the patience of a man watching an opponent trip over his own scabbard.)* …She did not do *that.* *(mildly)* The dramatic rescue you describe — the *fire*, the *twins* — that was a Goodwife *Harrow*, in '68, who *also* hangs in my files, and who *also* served no god, and whom we *also* walled. *(he makes a small note)* You have just argued, with great passion, for the innocence of *the wrong dead woman.* *(a thin sigh)* This is *precisely* why reckonings go *quickly*, friend. The defense *cannot keep the corpses straight*, and the *law* never has to. *Compose* yourself. The *living* one is in the *dock.* Try to argue for *her.*

nodes.speaker: Brother Sevard
nodes.text: *(Something flickers behind his eyes — the particular discomfort of a precise man hearing his own imprecision named.)* …A *state of being*, not an *act.* *(slowly)* You have put your finger on the thing I am *trained* not to look at. *(he turns the indictment so the single line faces him)* *'Found to have rendered no service…'* — you are *right*, it is *passive*, it is a *negative*, it charges her with an *absence.* We do not say *she stole*, *she killed*, *she broke a vow.* We say *she failed to kneel.* *(his voice drops)* And you are *right* that no *other* court in the Realms would call an *absence* a *crime.* If a baker bakes no bread *for a god*, we do not *hang* him; we note he is no *priest.* *(he sets the page down)* But the Wall is not *another court.* It is the *one* court built to punish *exactly* this — the *absence* of binding — because the *Concord* needs the *unbound* for its *mortar.* *(he meets your eyes, and there is something almost like shame)* You have not shown me she is *innocent.* You have shown me the *charge* is *monstrous on its face* — which I *knew*, and *filed away*, eleven years ago, so I could keep *reading* it. *(quietly)* Take *that* to the reeve. The old man trained as a *magistrate*, before the Choir. He *knows* the difference between a *crime* and a *category.* Make him *remember* it. I'll not *stop* you.

nodes.speaker: Brother Sevard
nodes.text: *(He waits out your argument and answers with the smooth ease of a man on home ground.)* …*Being* versus *doing.* *(a small nod)* A fine distinction in a *philosophy* hall. But the Concord does not *recognize* it, friend, and *I* am bound to the Concord, not to your *seminar.* *(he taps the line)* The law says *Faithless* is forfeit. It does not *ask* whether Faithlessness is a *deed* or a *disposition.* It does not *care.* *(a thin smile)* You are trying to win on a *technicality* of *grammar.* The Wall does not read *grammar.* It reads the *one bit*: bound, or unbound. She is unbound. *(he dips the pen)* Bring me something the *Concord* recognizes, or bring me nothing. *Cleverness* is not *standing.*

nodes.speaker: Brother Sevard
nodes.text: *(The pen stops dead. For the first time, the unhurried certainty is simply *gone*, and what replaces it is the white, careful stillness of a clerk who has just heard the foundation of his entire profession named as a fraud — and who, being a clerk, knows at once that it is *true.*)* …*Venn.* *(barely)* You said *Venn.* *(he does not pretend not to know the name; that would be beneath a thorough man)* The *Founding Concord* is sealed *V-E-N-N*, in the *clerk's* hand, in the *archive* I am *forbidden* to open and have *opened* anyway, because I am *thorough*, and the *thoroughness* is a *curse.* *(he sits, heavily)* I told myself it was a *scribe's* mark. A *witness.* A *secretary* signing *for* the Power. *(he looks at you, stricken)* You are telling me it was the *Power's* signature *itself* — that there *was* no god's hand, only a *living man's*, *forging* the authority to *wall up his neighbours.* *(a long, awful silence)* …Four centuries. *Eleven years* of *mine.* On a *forgery.* *(his voice is almost inaudible)* I cannot *prosecute* this, friend. Not *now.* Not *knowing.* A *case* rests on its *instrument*, and the *instrument* is a *crime.* *(he squares the indictment one last time, and then, deliberately, tears it in half)* The prosecution *withdraws*, for *want of a valid Concord.* *(he stands)* I am going to the *archive.* I am going to read *every* seal. And if they are *all* Venn's hand — *(he meets your eyes, hollow and resolute)* — then I have a great many *reckonings* to *un-write*, and not *nearly* enough *life* to do it in. *Go.* Tell the old woman the *law* that *damned* her was *never law at all.*

nodes.speaker: Brother Sevard
nodes.text: *(He goes very still. The suggestion that he send a runner to verify his superior's faith is one he clearly does not want to act on — because some part of him already knows the answer.)* …Send a runner. *(quietly)* To ask the *High Measurer* whether she still *vouches* for the *ledger.* *(a long pause; he does not call for a runner)* …I will be *honest* with you, friend, because dishonesty is *untidy*: I do not *need* to send the runner. *(he sets down the pen)* I *felt* it, when the word came down this evening — that the Canon had been *shaken*, that she'd locked her office and dismissed the scriveners and not come out. *(he looks at the dock)* In *eleven years* Canon Mereth has been the *fixed star* I *navigated* by. If *she* has stopped *believing* the books — and your face tells me she *has* — then I am a man taking a *bearing* off a star that has *gone out*, and *still steering by it* because I do not know *how* to steer by anything *else.* *(a dry, mirthless breath)* …I will not pronounce a *forfeiture* tonight on the authority of a superior in the middle of a *crisis of faith.* That would not be *thorough.* It would be *cowardice* wearing *thoroughness* as a *coat.* *(he addresses the reeve's bench)* The prosecution *requests a stay* — pending the High Measurer's *reaffirmation* of the founding ledger. *(to you, low)* She will *never* reaffirm it. You *saw* to that. Which means this reckoning is *stayed* until a *thawing* glacier *re-freezes.* *(the ghost of something not unlike relief)* …The midwife *walks*, friend. On a *technicality* you *manufactured* upstairs. *Clever.* …And *merciful*, which I had *forgotten* was *allowed.*

nodes.speaker: Brother Sevard
nodes.text: *(He does not answer at once. He looks at the cold coming off you — really looks — and the thin certainty in his face contends with something he has spent eleven years never having to face: a witness to the *other end* of his own paperwork.)* …Under oath. *(slowly)* You would *describe* it. *Forfeiture.* From the *inside.* *(he swallows)* Do you understand what you are *asking*, friend? In *eleven years* of reckonings, no soul has *ever* testified to what the *sentence* is *like*, because no soul has *ever* come *back* from it to *say.* The *phrase* — *'forfeit to the Wall'* — works *precisely* because it is a *closed door.* We say it, the door shuts, and what is *behind* the door is *no one's* business because no one *returns* from behind it. *(he is silent a moment)* …And you are proposing to *open the door* in the *middle of my courtroom* and let the *reeve* look *through* it. *(a long breath)* …I cannot *stop* you. A *court* must *hear* a *witness*, and you are the *only* witness in the *history* of the Wall *competent* to speak to the *sentence itself.* *(he sets down his pen, defeated and, strangely, freed)* …*Speak*, then, Returned. Tell the record what is on the *other side* of my *tidy phrase.* *(quietly)* I have *wondered* for *eleven years*. God help me, I think I *need* to *hear* it as much as the *reeve* does. And after — *(he cannot finish)* — after, I do not think I will be *able* to read the *line.* Which is, I *suspect*, exactly what you *intend.*

nodes.speaker: Brother Sevard
nodes.text: *(He weighs the threat with the cool detachment of a man pricing a risk, and the arithmetic comes out against him.)* …A *gallery* of *waking* mothers. *(flatly)* In the *Counting-House.* At *night.* *(he sets down the pen)* You understand the *whole design* of a reckoning is its *quiet* — no *witnesses*, no *grief*, no *trouble*, a *form* completed in the *dark* and *filed* before *dawn.* *(a thin, sour line of a mouth)* A *mob* of *grateful* mothers, *weeping*, *naming* the children this woman *caught* — that is not a *form.* That is an *incident.* And *incidents* go in *reports*, and *reports* go *up*, and the *Canon* — *(he stops)* — the Canon is in *no state* to receive an *incident* tonight, by all accounts. *(he exhales through his nose)* …You have found the *one* lever that moves a *bureaucrat*, friend: not *justice*, but *paperwork he does not want to file.* *(he picks up the indictment and sets it aside, unread)* The reckoning is *adjourned.* *Indefinitely.* For *want of a convenient hour.* *(a cold look)* Do not *mistake* this for *mercy.* I am not *sparing* her. I am *avoiding* a *Tuesday* I do not wish to *have.* …But she *walks.* For *tonight*, and — knowing how *adjournments* *rot* in this place — likely for *good.* *Take* her, and *go*, before I *find* the *nerve* I have just *misplaced.*

nodes.speaker: Brother Sevard
nodes.text: *(✦ You don't just threaten a mob — you describe it, precisely, person by person: the names of the children, the faces of the mothers, the exact words they'll cry in his tidy dark room, and then you tell him you'll have it *transcribed* and *posted* on the Counting-House door by dawn, so that every soul who passes learns what the Choir does at night. You threaten not his body but the one thing a clerk cannot survive: a *public record* of his own cruelty.)* *(All the colour leaves his face.)* …*Posted.* *(barely)* On the *door.* With the *names.* *(he sits down hard)* You — you would make a *document* of it. A *true* one. *Transcribed.* *Public.* *(he understands, with a clerk's horror, that this is the one weapon against which he has no defense, because he has spent his life *believing in documents*)* A *reckoning* survives on being *unwritten-of* — a thing that *happens* and is *filed* and is *never spoken.* And you would *write of it.* *Loudly.* In the *one* hand the Choir *cannot* discredit: the *plain account of what was done.* *(he is shaking, very slightly)* I withdraw. *(he tears the indictment, hands unsteady)* I withdraw, and I will *strike* this reckoning from the *night-book* as *never convened*, and you will *not* post your document, and we will *both* pretend this *room* was *empty* tonight. *(he looks at you with something between terror and awe)* …You frighten me more than the *Wall* does, Returned. The *Wall* only *un-makes* a soul. *You* would have *un-made my deniability.* *Take* the midwife. *Burn* the transcript. And *never* tell me your *name* — I do not want it in any record *I* might one day have to *answer* for.

nodes.speaker: Brother Sevard
nodes.text: *(He listens to the threat and, to your surprise, the thin certainty does not waver — it *settles.*)* …You'll wake the mothers. *(mildly)* Bring a *mob.* Make it *loud.* *(he folds his hands)* Friend, I have *measured* for the Wall for *eleven years.* Do you imagine you are the *first* to threaten a *scene?* They *weep*, sometimes — the *living*, when they *learn.* They *shout.* They *come* with *torches.* *(a thin, tired smile)* And then the *Justiciars* come, because a *mob* in the *Counting-House* is a *breach of the Concord's peace*, and the *mob* goes to *cells*, and the *reckoning* proceeds *anyway*, only now with *more* souls in the *dock.* *(he picks the pen back up)* You cannot *frighten* a *machine*, friend. You can only *feed* it. *Threaten* me again, and I will add *'incited disorder at a reckoning'* to the *night's* business, and find *room* on the *Wall* for *two.* *(coolly)* Make your *argument*, or take your *leave.* But do not make your *threats.* I have *outlasted* better.

nodes.speaker: Brother Sevard
nodes.text: *(He inclines his head, unbothered, and draws the indictment back in front of him.)* As you wish. *(mildly)* The *line* will be here when you *return* — if you return. *(he dips the pen)* It is *always* here. That is the *whole* comfort of the *law*, friend: it does not *tire*, it does not *doubt*, it does not *wander* off to *brood* by the *braziers.* It simply *waits*, in its *single* grey sentence, for the *night* to grow *short* enough that *no one* is left to *argue.* *(he does not look up)* I have *all* night. The *midwife* has rather *less.* *Mind* the *hour*, spectator. It is the *one* witness that *never* takes *my* side and *always* takes the *Wall's.*

nodes.speaker: Brother Sevard
nodes.text: *(💀 You go for the menacing ultimatum and, at the crucial moment, gesture so broadly that you knock the prosecutor's inkwell across his squared indictment — and then, attempting to recover your menace while mopping ink with your sleeve, deliver the threat in a register that comes out closer to an apology. Brother Sevard watches a black tide consume his paperwork with an expression of profound, weary calm.)* …You have *spilled* my *ink.* *(flatly)* On the *indictment.* While *threatening* me. *(he lifts the ruined page by one corner, dripping)* …Well. *(a long-suffering sigh)* I suppose I must *re-copy* it now. *(he sets it aside)* Which will take the better part of an *hour*, during which you might have *mounted* an *actual* defense, but instead you have given me *fresh* stationery and *second* thoughts about your *competence.* *(he draws a clean sheet)* Sit *down*, friend. *Compose* yourself. And keep your *elbows* off the *evidence.* A *court* is no place for *flailing* — that is what the *Wall* is *for.*

variants.text: *(The old reeve looks down at you from the bench, and where you expected weariness there is something startled and almost *young* — a man who came in tonight to pronounce a sentence and has, instead, watched his own prosecutor *withdraw.*)* …In *thirty years* on this bench. *(he sets the gavel down, very gently, as though it might go off)* Thirty years, I have *never* seen Brother Sevard *fold.* The man is *granite.* And you — *(he studies you)* — you walked into my *empty* court and turned my *granite* prosecutor into a man *tearing up his own indictment.* *(he exhales)* I do not *know* yet whether to *thank* you or *fear* you. *Speak.* I have a *pronouncement* to make, and for the first time in *thirty years* I do not know which *way* it falls.

*(The old reeve regards you with the bottomless weariness of a man who has said the same six words — *forfeit to the Wall, so reckoned* — more times than he can bear to count, and stopped hearing them somewhere around the thousandth.)* …A spectator. *(the gavel turns slowly in his hand)* We do not *get* spectators. The reckonings are *quiet.* That is the *kindness* in them, or so I *told* myself, the first *decade.* *(he looks at the dock, at Annet, and away)* I am Reeve Oddleif. I *pronounce.* That is *all* I do now — the Measurer *reads*, the scale *settles*, and I bring down the *gavel* and say the *words* and another *soul* goes into the *stone.* *(a long, grey pause)* I was a *magistrate*, once. *Before.* I *weighed* things, then. *Evidence.* *Mitigation.* *Doubt.* *(he looks at the gavel as though it were a stranger's hand)* …Why are you *here*, cold one? Reckonings have *no gallery.* And yet you *came.* Speak quickly. The *night* is *short*, and the *Wall* is *patient*, and I am *neither.*

nodes.speaker: Reeve Oddleif
nodes.text: *(He waits, gavel still, the weight of thirty years and one strange night sitting on the bench beside him.)* Say your piece, cold one. *(quietly)* I am *listening* — which is, I will tell you, more than I have *done* on this bench in a *very* long while.

choices.text: *(if the prosecution has collapsed)* "Your prosecutor withdrew, Reeve. There is no indictment left to pronounce on — only a godless midwife who caught half this city's children, standing in a dock with nothing valid against her. You were a magistrate once. You know what a magistrate does with a case that has no charge. *Acquit* her. Strike the gavel for the *living*, for once."

[PERSUASION] "You weighed things once, Reeve. Evidence. Mitigation. Doubt. The Choir took your scales and gave you a rubber stamp, and you've called the numbness 'kindness' for twenty years. Here's your chance to be a *magistrate* again, just once, before the end. Weigh this woman honestly — not against a god, against the *good she did* — and pronounce what a *just* man would."

*(one who's seen what the gavel sends souls to)* "Before you strike that, Reeve — know where it sends her. I've been inside the Wall. The word you pronounce isn't a death. It's a slow un-making, a soul kept awake and coming apart for centuries. You've said those six words a thousand times without knowing what they *do.* Now you know. Strike the gavel anyway, if you can."
"What's stopping you, Reeve? You were a magistrate. You know this is wrong. What does the Choir hold over an old man that he'd pronounce a sentence he stopped believing in twenty years ago?"
"I'll let you weigh it, Reeve. Take your time. It's been a while."

nodes.speaker: Reeve Oddleif
nodes.text: *(He looks at the torn indictment on the prosecutor's empty desk, and then at the gavel in his hand, and then — slowly, wonderingly — he sets the gavel *down*, and does not pick it back up.)* …No charge. *(barely)* A magistrate, with no charge before him, *dismisses.* *(he says it like a catechism half-remembered from a former life)* It is the *first* thing they teach you, and the *last* thing I *forgot.* *(he rises — the whole court seems to hold its breath — and addresses the dock directly, which no reeve does)* Goodwife Annet. *(his voice cracks on thirty years of disuse)* The prosecution against you is *withdrawn.* There being *no valid charge* before this court, I find I have *nothing to pronounce* — and a reeve who pronounces on *nothing* is not a reeve, he is a *machine*, and I was a *man* once, and I *propose* to *die* one. *(he strikes the gavel — but lightly, like a *blessing* instead of a *sentence*)* You are *free.* Go and catch *more* of this city's children. The Realms have *too few* who'll do it for *no god's* thanks. *(to you, low and shaken)* …Thirty years, cold one. Thirty years of *six words.* And the *thirty-first* I get to say *different* ones. *(his eyes are wet)* I do not know what they will *do* to me, for this. I find I do not *care.* You have *handed an old machine back its* *humanity*, on the *one* night it had nearly *forgotten* it had any. *Thank* you. *Gods* — or *no* gods, like the *brave* old woman — *thank* you.

nodes.speaker: Reeve Oddleif
nodes.text: *(The word *magistrate* lands like a key in a lock rusted thirty years shut. He is silent a long time, and when he speaks, the grey procedural voice is gone, and under it is the man he buried to keep his post.)* …A *magistrate.* *(roughly)* I have not let myself be *called* that since I *took the grey.* *(he sets the gavel across his knees and *looks* at it)* They *recruited* me *for* it, you know — *'a trained legal mind lends the reckonings dignity'* — and I told myself the *dignity* was *real*, that a soul deserved a *proper* pronouncement even on the way to the *stone*, that I was a *kindness* in the *machine.* *(his jaw works)* It took me *ten* years to see I was the *machine's* best *disguise.* That the *dignity* was the *grease.* That every *grave* word I spoke made the *atrocity* go down *smoother.* *(he looks at Annet, and then at you, and something old straightens in him)* …You ask me to *weigh* her. *Honestly.* Against the *good she did.* *(a long breath)* The Choir will *break* me for it. But I am an *old* man, and they can only break what I have *left*, and what I have *left*, it turns out, is *exactly* enough to do this *once.* *(he rises)* I will *weigh* her *true*, cold one — and a *true* scale, for *this* woman, comes down so far on the side of *mercy* that the *Wall* does not *enter* into it. *Go.* Stand by the *dock.* You'll want to *see* an old magistrate's *last honest verdict.*

nodes.speaker: Reeve Oddleif
nodes.text: *(✦ You don't just call him a magistrate — you ask him the question he buried thirty years ago: how many of the thousand he sentenced were *Annets.* Midwives, healers, kind godless souls who served the living and were mortared for the absence of a kneeling. You make him do, out loud, the arithmetic of his own career — and a man can survive being numb, but he cannot survive *counting.*)* *(The gavel slips from his hand and clatters on the bench.)* …How *many.* *(a whisper)* You ask me how *many* were *Annets.* *(his face does something terrible)* I do not — I made myself *stop* seeing them as *people* in the *first decade*, precisely so I would *never* have to *count* — and now you have *asked*, and the number is — *(his voice breaks completely)* — the number is *most* of them, cold one. *Most.* The *cruel* ones, the *truly* faithless-of-*heart* — those were *rare.* It was *mostly* *midwives.* *Healers.* *Herbwives* and *ferrymen* and *folk* who served *people* instead of *Powers* and got *walled* for the *distinction.* *A thousand Annets.* *(he is weeping openly now, an old man undone by his own ledger)* I cannot give the *thousand* back. *(he picks up the gavel with a shaking hand)* But I can refuse to make it a *thousand and one.* *(he stands, and his voice, through the tears, finds an old courtroom iron it has not had in thirty years)* This reckoning is *dismissed*, the charge is *void*, the accused is *free* — and as my *last* act on this *bench*, which I now *vacate*, I enter into the *record* that *Reeve Oddleif* *recants* *every* pronouncement he made in the service of the *Wall*, and names them, all *thousand*, *murders*, and *signs* it. *(he looks at you, wrecked and luminous)* Let the *Choir* do what it *will* to an old man. I have just become, for *one night*, the *magistrate* they *hired* me to *impersonate.* Take the midwife. And, cold one — *find* her a *witness* who'll write down what *happened* here. I want the *thousand* *remembered.* It is *all* the *justice* I have *left* to *give* them.

nodes.speaker: Reeve Oddleif
nodes.text: *(He hears you out, and the old weariness closes back over the flicker you almost reached.)* …A *magistrate.* *(he shakes his head slowly)* You reach for a man who *died*, cold one, thirty years gone, in a grey room with a *gavel* in his hand. *(he turns it over)* I *know* it is wrong. *Knowing* is not the *trouble.* I have *known* it was wrong for *twenty* years, and *known* is *exactly* as far as it *goes*, because *knowing* costs *nothing* and *acting* costs *everything*, and I am an *old* man with *little* left and a great *fear* of losing even *that.* *(quietly)* You will have to do *better* than *remind* me what I *was.* I *miss* him every day, the magistrate. *Missing* him has never once been *enough* to *resurrect* him. *(he lifts the gavel)* Give me a *reason* that outweighs my *fear*, or stand aside and let me do the *cowardly* thing I have done a *thousand* times. It is *late*, and *fear* is *heavy*, and I am *tired* of *holding* it up alone.

nodes.speaker: Reeve Oddleif
nodes.text: *(Reaching for gravitas, you address the old jurist with a stirring invocation of 'the ancient and noble office of the reeve' — except you call him by the wrong name, twice, and attribute to him a famous judgement rendered by a magistrate in an entirely different city two hundred years before he was born. The old man's eyebrows climb.)* …I am not *Reeve Haldane.* *(dryly)* Reeve *Haldane* sat in *Waterdeep*, ruled on the *Cormyr* grain case, and has been *dead* since before my *grandmother* was *churched.* *(he sighs)* I am Reeve *Oddleif*, I sit in a *cellar* under the *Choir*, and I have ruled on *nothing* of note in *thirty* years but the *steady* damnation of the *poor.* *(a tired, almost amused look)* If you mean to *flatter* an old man onto your *side*, cold one, it helps to flatter the *correct* old man, *living*, and in the *room.* *(he settles back)* Gather your *wits.* And your *names.* I'll not be *moved* by a *speech* meant for *someone else.* I have heard *quite* enough words meant for *someone else* in this *grey* room.

nodes.speaker: Reeve Oddleif
nodes.text: *(He leans forward, and the gavel goes still in his hand, and he searches your face the way he must once, long ago, have searched a witness's for the truth.)* …A *slow un-making.* *(quietly)* A soul kept *awake.* *(he sets the gavel down)* I have *suspected* it. *(his voice is very low)* You do not sit *thirty* years above that *scale* without coming to *feel* the cold that leaks up through the *floor* from the *stone* below — and to *wonder*, in the *worst* of the *small* hours, whether the *six* words I say are a *door* I *shut* on something that *keeps screaming* on the *other* side. *(he looks at you, stricken)* I told myself it was *quick.* I *needed* it to be *quick.* And you are telling me, you who *walked back out of it*, that it is *not* an ending at *all* — that I have *pronounced* a *thousand* souls into a *waking* dissolution and called it a *sentence.* *(his hands shake)* …Then I have not been a *reeve*, cold one. I have been a *torturer* with *good diction.* *(he pushes the gavel away across the bench)* The accused is *free.* I will *pronounce* no *forfeiture* tonight, nor *any* night hereafter, and they may *fetch* a *younger* reeve who has not yet *met* a soul that *came back* to tell him what the *word* means. *(barely)* You have *ruined* me for the *work*, Returned. It is the *kindest* thing anyone has done me in *thirty* years.

nodes.speaker: Reeve Oddleif
nodes.text: *(The question lands somewhere old and sore, and he is quiet a while before he answers it honestly.)* …What do they *hold* over me. *(a grey, tired breath)* It is not what you'd *think.* No *threat* to my *kin* — I have *outlived* my *kin.* *(he turns the gavel)* What holds me is *smaller* and *worse* than any threat. It is a *pension.* A *warm* room. The *quiet* certainty of a *fed* old age, in exchange for *six* words a *night.* *(his mouth twists)* They did not *coerce* me. They *bought* me — *cheaply* — with the *one* thing an old man fears losing more than his *soul*: his *comfort.* *(he looks at his hands)* I tell myself I *stay* to lend the reckonings *dignity.* The *truth* is the *bench* is *warm* and the *alternative* is *cold*, and I am *old.* *(he meets your eyes, self-loathing and the faint dangerous beginning of resolve in it)* …What *holds* me is *nothing* a *braver* man wouldn't *drop* in an *instant.* *Make* me braver, cold one. *Quickly.* Before the *warm* room *wins* again, the way it has *won* for *twenty* years.

nodes.speaker: Reeve Oddleif
nodes.text: *(He nods slowly, and turns the gavel over in his old hands, and does not, you notice, lift it.)* …Take my *time.* *(a grey, faraway voice)* I have *taken* none, in *thirty* years. The Measurer *reads*, the scale *settles*, the gavel *falls* — *quick*, before a man can *think.* That is the *design.* *(he studies the dock, the brave old woman, the empty gallery, you)* …Perhaps I will *take* some, tonight. It has been a *long* while since a *reckoning* had anything in it *worth* taking time *over.* *(he sets the gavel down, just for a moment)* …*Time.* Yes. Let me *weigh* it. The way I *used* to. *(half to himself)* …I had *forgotten* the *bench* could be *used* for *weighing.* I thought it was *only* for *dropping.*
